title: if and switch

publication.changeSummary: Language-specific C: Beginner to Intermediate draft for if and switch; human standards, technical, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: GNU C Language Introduction and Reference Manual; accessed 2026-07; provisions applicable to if and switch
sources.claim: C selection uses scalar conditions where zero is false and nonzero is true, with if/else binding and switch case labels determining one control path.

canonicalExplanation.summary: C selection uses scalar conditions where zero is false and nonzero is true, with if/else binding and switch case labels determining one control path.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: An if/else-if chain handles negative, zero, and positive values in mutually exclusive branches and tests both boundaries. Your goal is to implement and test boundary-complete if and switch logic. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Operators and precedence; pf control iteration. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Language rule and operational model
sections.body: C selection uses scalar conditions where zero is false and nonzero is true, with if/else binding and switch case labels determining one control path. This rule is applied to concrete C source and observable behavior, not treated as a vocabulary definition alone.

sections.heading: Boundary and common failure
sections.body: Floating comparisons, fallthrough, duplicate or unreachable cases, dangling else layout, and side effects in conditions can make branches surprising. Therefore the draft explicitly rejects this beginner failure model: Every switch case stops automatically when its statements finish, even without break or another control transfer.

includes: Express decisions with Boolean conditions and integral dispatch.
Implement and test boundary-complete if and switch logic.
excludes: Floating comparisons, fallthrough, duplicate or unreachable cases, dangling else layout, and side effects in conditions can make branches surprising.

outcomes: Implement and test boundary-complete if and switch logic.

examples.title: if and switch: defined case
examples.situation: An if/else-if chain handles negative, zero, and positive values in mutually exclusive branches and tests both boundaries.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Inspect the concrete source or state: An if/else-if chain handles negative, zero, and positive values in mutually exclusive branches and tests both boundaries.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the conclusion: A switch continues into following labels unless control transfers; use break or an explicit documented fallthrough when that behavior is intended.
examples.result: The defined result follows because A switch continues into following labels unless control transfers; use break or an explicit documented fallthrough when that behavior is intended.

examples.title: if and switch: boundary case
examples.situation: A switch groups two case labels intentionally and uses break to prevent execution from falling into the next unrelated action.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Identify the changed source or state: A switch groups two case labels intentionally and uses break to prevent execution from falling into the next unrelated action.
Step 2 — Keep this standard boundary visible: Floating comparisons, fallthrough, duplicate or unreachable cases, dangling else layout, and side effects in conditions can make branches surprising.
Step 3 — Reject the tempting rule: Every switch case stops automatically when its statements finish, even without break or another control transfer.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Implement and test boundary-complete if and switch logic.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: Every switch case stops automatically when its statements finish, even without break or another control transfer.
misconceptions.correction: A switch continues into following labels unless control transfers; use break or an explicit documented fallthrough when that behavior is intended.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Using concrete C rules, explain why this belief fails: Every switch case stops automatically when its statements finish, even without break or another control transfer.

analogy.example: A manually managed workshop can model if and switch: every tool, address, buffer, and acquired resource has an explicit valid range and owner.
usefulFor: Making low-level state, bounds, representation, ownership, and cleanup responsibilities concrete
limitations: C abstract-machine rules, undefined behavior, pointer provenance, translation, and implementation variation are stricter than a physical workshop analogy.
Floating comparisons, fallthrough, duplicate or unreachable cases, dangling else layout, and side effects in conditions can make branches surprising.

recap.summary: You should now be able to implement and test boundary-complete if and switch logic. Keep this boundary in view: Floating comparisons, fallthrough, duplicate or unreachable cases, dangling else layout, and side effects in conditions can make branches surprising.
retrievalPrompts: Explain if and switch using a concrete C rule rather than the optional analogy.
Why is this claim incorrect: Every switch case stops automatically when its statements finish, even without break or another control transfer.
Apply the checkpoint 'break' to the boundary case from memory.
recap.nextReviewPrompt: Next: continue to “while, do and for”. Before moving on, answer one retrieval prompt without reopening the explanation.
